subject: Immigrant children with Red Cross port workers at Pier 21, Halifax, Nova Scotia, Canada, 1948
context_for_lesson: A 1948 photograph showing immigrant children being greeted and assisted by Canadian Red Cross workers at Pier 21, Halifax. The Canadian Red Cross provided port-side aid — food, clothing, language interpretation, and temporary shelter — to the hundreds of thousands of displaced persons and refugees who passed through Pier 21 in the late 1940s. This image captures one of the gentler moments in the Canadian arrival experience, a moment Judy Abrams (who arrived in 1949) likely lived through. Powerful for showing children who survived the Holocaust beginning a new life.
publisher_or_host: Wikimedia Commons (originally from Nova Scotia Archives, accession no. 1975-305 item 4812-4)
author: E.A. Bollinger
license_text_verbatim: This Canadian work is in the public domain in Canada because its copyright has expired due to one of the following: 1. it was subject to Crown copyright and was first published more than 50 years ago, or it was not subject to Crown copyright, and 2. it is a photograph that was created prior to January 1, 1949.
license_summary: Public Domain in Canada and the United States. Free to use for any purpose including commercial, with no attribution required (though attribution is given here).
attribution_required: Photo: E.A. Bollinger, Nova Scotia Archives, accession no. 1975-305 item 4812-4 (Public Domain in Canada)
lesson_use_intent: Page 7 (journey-map page) — Montreal-1949 waypoint, OR a sidebar/closing panel showing 'Arrival in Canada'. Caption MUST clarify: 'Pier 21, Halifax, 1948. Canadian Red Cross workers welcoming refugee children at Canada's main port of arrival. Judy Abrams arrived in Canada in 1949, likely passing through this same arrival hall. (This is not Judy.)' Do NOT imply this is Judy.
concerns: Caption must clarify this is not Judy — general historical context for postwar Jewish refugee arrival in Canada. Strong pedagogical fit because it shows children being met with care, an emotional anchor for the lesson's resolution.
